text: This video shows laparoscopic repair of colonic perforation following removal of the colonic polyp. In an eighty one year old male. He had severe abdominal pain in the recovery with abdominal distension and dropped his blood pressure and he was peritoneitic. CT scan shows large amount of free air.

The surgeon releases free air through the umbilical port and establishes pneumoperitoneum at twelve millimetres of mercury. Three additional laparoscopic ports are inserted: a ten-millimetre port at the umbilicus, a five-millimetre port at the left iliac fossa, and a five-millimetre port at the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon explores and assesses the site of perforation on the transverse colon, where a tattoo marking is visible. The decision is made to repair the perforation rather than resect, based on the endoscopist's assessment of the polyp as benign.

The surgeon assesses an AAST grade II injury on the colon, measuring less than four centimeters in length. The area is well vascularized and mobile, with minimal contamination due to the patient's prepared bowel. The margin of the perforation is being freshened.
The surgeon uses a 4x3 inch swab to clean the margin of the colonic perforation, soaking up blood from the area.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing of the colonic perforation using 3-0 PDS sutures. The first two interrupted, full-thickness sutures are placed at the far and near ends of the perforation. These sutures are then pulled out through the 10mm port on the right side to act as stay sutures. Three additional sutures are placed to complete the repair.

The surgeon places an additional suture at the furthermost end of the perforation site on the transverse colon. Intracorporeal knots are tied off, starting with the far end suture, followed by the near end, and finally the middle sutures.
The surgeon ties off the first two sutures with the needle attached, then removes the needle for subsequent sutures, facilitating easier intracorporeal knot tying using the C and reverse C technique.

The surgeon begins the Thorola Bhaj procedure, starting around the site of the perforation, then moving towards the left upper quadrant, central inter-loop area, pelvis, right side of the abdomen, right sub-hepatic, and right supranic areas. A size twenty-eight Wallis drain is placed on the left side of the abdomen and secured.

Apart from confusion, his post-operative period was uneventful. and he went home on day seven post-op. The histology of the polyp was a cell serrated lesion without any dysplasia and he remains well at three months follow-up.
Thank you for watching this video.
